coredata.dc:title: Evolution from UV emission of phenyl groups to visible emission of pyrolytic nanocarbons dispersed in fumed silica: Alternative insight into photoluminescence of carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Vasin, A.V.
Muto, S.
Ishikawa, Y.
Kysil, D.V.
Sevostianov, S.V.
Isaieva, O.F.
Rudko, G. Yu.
Yatskiv, R.
Starik, S.
Tertykh, V.A.
Nazarov, A.N.
Lysenko, V.S.
coredata.dc:description: SiO2:C nanopowder, luminescent in ultraviolet and visible spectral ranges, was synthesised using fumed silica as morphological matrix (specific surface of 295 m2/g) and phenyltrimethoxysilane (PhTMS) as carbon precursor. Fumed silica was impregnated by toluene solution of PhTMS followed by drying and annealing at 600 °C. Two series of SiO2:C nanocomposites with different carbon concentration were synthesised and studied. The main idea of the study was to explore the correlation of structural reconstruction and evolution of photoluminescent properties of carbon moieties. Chemically modified fumed silica exhibited ultraviolet (UV) photoluminescence band at about 330 nm originated from phenyl groups. After calcinations at 600 °C two overlapped emission bands were found at about 380 nm and 420 nm in “low-carbon” SiO2:C, while “high-carbon” series exhibited broad but homogeneous emission band peaked at about 440–450 nm. One more characteristic emission band at 495 nm in SiO2:C appeared and developed under intense ultraviolet radiation. Evolution of light emission from ultraviolet to visible was analysed in correlation with structural reconstruction of carbon examined by FTIR spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, high resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy, and electron energy loss spectroscopy. The nature of light emission of pyrolytic carbon moieties in SiO2:C nanopowder was discussed in frame of the concepts of polycyclic carbon clusters and their aggregates based on the analogy with polycyclic organic molecules and luminescent carbon nanodots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Fumed silica
Carbon nanodots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of structure and chemical composition
Carbon mapping and analysis of C–K edge

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Center in Ukraine
Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education and Science of Ukraine
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education and Science of Ukraine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by Science&Technology Center in Ukraine ( Project #6362 ) and Ministry of Education and Science of Ukraine ( Project F2904 ). One of the authors (AVV) acknowledges the 27th (2018) DAIKO ZAIDAN Scholarship for the international collaboration program by inviting foreign researchers to Japanese research institutes. This work was in part supported by Nagoya University microstructural characterization platform as a program of “Nanotechnology Platform” of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT), Japan .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical emission spectrum and spectral characteristics of the light source based on 401 nm LED and SiO2:C phosphor.
Emission and excitation spectra of as-prepared LC (a, b) and HC (c, d) phenylsilicas.

Emission and excitation spectra of annealed LC (a, b) and HC (c, d) phenylsilicas. (e) – peak positions of PL peak in LC and HC series as function of excitation wavelength. (f) – emission spectrum of LC sample (in energy scale) and corresponding fitting by four Gauss components centered at 2.8 eV (443 nm), 3.0 eV (413 nm), 3.3 eV (375 nm), and 3.7 eV (335 nm).

(a) - Normalised emission spectra of HC phenylsilica annealed at “gradient temperature” conditions. (b) - Fitting of the PL spectrum (λ EXC = 280 nm) by four Gauss components: 2.7 eV (459 nm), 3.0 eV (413 nm), 3.4 eV (365 nm), and 3.8 eV (326 nm).

(a) - PL spectra of LC sample under 325 nm laser excitation with varying radiation power; (b) - PL spectra measured using minimal radiation power (3 μW) at the beginning of experiment (spectrum 1) and after 2 h radiation (spectrum 2).
Typical variation of integral PL intensity of SiO2:C samples as a function of 325 nm laser irradiation time in air and in vacuum.

Selected spectral ranges of transmission FTIR spectra. (a) Spectral range of CC stretching modes: (1) pristine fumed silica, (2) LC phenylsilica, (3) HC phenylsilica, (4) LC annealed, (5) HC annealed. (b) Spectral range of C–H stretching modes: (1) LC phenylsilica, (2) HC phenylsilica, (3) LC annealed, (4) HC annealed.
(a,b) - C1s XPS peaks of annealed LC and HC series respectively. (c,d) - Si2p and O1s peaks of pristine fumed silica and annealed LC and HC series.
Typical dark field STEM images and corresponding maps of the spacial distribution of the Si, O and C elements in annealed HC series.

EELS C–K ionisation edge spectra of annealed LC and HC series (spectrum 1 and 2 respectively). For comparison it is presented C–K spectrum for graphene oxide flake, measured at similar conditions (spectrum 3).
Structure-energy scheme of light emitters in SiO2:C.

head.ce:title: Evolution from UV emission of phenyl groups to visible emission of pyrolytic nanocarbons dispersed in fumed silica: Alternative insight into photoluminescence of carbon nanodots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lashkaryov Institute of Semiconductor Physics of NASU

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: SiO2:C nanopowder, luminescent in ultraviolet and visible spectral ranges, was synthesised using fumed silica as morphological matrix (specific surface of 295 m2/g) and phenyltrimethoxysilane (PhTMS) as carbon precursor. Fumed silica was impregnated by toluene solution of PhTMS followed by drying and annealing at 600 °C. Two series of SiO2:C nanocomposites with different carbon concentration were synthesised and studied. The main idea of the study was to explore the correlation of structural reconstruction and evolution of photoluminescent properties of carbon moieties. Chemically modified fumed silica exhibited ultraviolet (UV) photoluminescence band at about 330 nm originated from phenyl groups. After calcinations at 600 °C two overlapped emission bands were found at about 380 nm and 420 nm in “low-carbon” SiO2:C, while “high-carbon” series exhibited broad but homogeneous emission band peaked at about 440–450 nm. One more characteristic emission band at 495 nm in SiO2:C appeared and developed under intense ultraviolet radiation. Evolution of light emission from ultraviolet to visible was analysed in correlation with structural reconstruction of carbon examined by FTIR spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, high resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy, and electron energy loss spectroscopy. The nature of light emission of pyrolytic carbon moieties in SiO2:C nanopowder was discussed in frame of the concepts of polycyclic carbon clusters and their aggregates based on the analogy with polycyclic organic molecules and luminescent carbon nanodots.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent materials, as part of light science and “technotronic” environment of the human are of great importance in modern optoelectronics, artificial lighting, and visualization/indication technologies [1]. The search for new light-emitting materials with enhanced functional properties and safe for human is an urgent task for research community nowadays. Nanostructured luminescent materials and nanoscale light emitters attract a special interest due to some specific advantages. Luminescent carbon nano-dots (CDs) is a new class of luminescent nanomaterials with efficient visible light emission [2,3] that is recently discovered and actively investigated nowadays with increasing activity. CDs is a wide class of carbon nanomaterials with moiety size of below 10 nm including molecular-like clusters/complexes, amorphous 3D nanoparticles, graphene and graphene oxide nanoflakes, etc. Because of relatively “young” age of the material there is still no commonly adopted terminology for identification of the structure and luminescence mechanisms. Some identifications of luminescent nanocarbons and their relations to semiconductor nanoparticles can be found in Ref. [4]. In frame of the present study the consideration is confined by carbon clusters and nanoparticles grown spontaneously through “bottom-up” root. Being biocompatible and having photoluminescence with large Stokes shift between emission and excitation bands, they have been already recognised as new segment of fluorescent nano-labels for nanoscale visualization in biomedical experiments [5]. Some researchers reported external quantum yield (QY) of photoluminescence of CDs diluted in ethanol or aqueous solutions to be above 90% [6–9], i.e. similar to that of the best inorganic phosphors. High QY and broad visible emission spectrum makes CDs highly attractive also for optoelectronic applications, for instance as up-converting phosphor for artificial lighting and other white light sources. However, CDs with effective PL are commonly synthesised by “wet chemistry” processes in form of colloidal solutions that is not compatible with optoelectronic devices. PL efficiency in extracted and dried solid state CDs is strongly reduced, presumably due to aggregation quenching effects similar to that in conjugated organic molecules.

The reasonable idea for avoiding undesirable aggregation of carbon nanoemitters is a proper dispersion of CDs into optically transparent solid-state matrix. Organic polymer matrix seems to be a good candidate and distinct progress in development of solid state upconverter was reported [10–13]. One of inorganic alternatives to polymers is nanostructured silicas with high specific area. Indeed, fumed silica nanopowder with specific surface area of 295 m2/g was successfully used as morphological matrix for synthesis of luminescent SiO2:C nanocomposite using tetramethoxysilane (TMS) and phenyltrimethoxysilane (PhTMS) as precursor of luminescent pyrolytical nanocarbons [14–16]. Fig. 1 demonstrates spectral properties of 401 nm LED excited SiO2:C phosphor on the base of carbonised fumed silica. Such white light source exhibits high colour rendering index, however the quantum yield of the phosphor is about 11% that is not sufficient for the practical application. Further development of the PL efficiency and optimisation of the material need better understanding of the physical mechanisms responsible for light absorption and emission. For this reason the structural identification of the nanoemitters is vital. However, despite a large number of publications on synthesis methods and PL properties of CDs there is still no commonly recognised structural model and corresponding physical mechanisms responsible for effective light emission in CDs. The researchers mainly focuse on description of synthesis and optical properties of the CDs avoiding identification of the structure. An impressive variety of the synthesis methods and rare organic materials used for the synthesis of CDs (including fruits, vegetables, coffee etc.) suggests that formation of luminescent nanocarbon moieties, commonly identified as carbon nanodots, is rather universal property of the processes of spontaneous precipitation of carbon. In the present work the evolution of the photoluminescence in SiO2:C system from ultraviolet photoluminescence of phenyl groups to visible emission of pyrolytical carbon nanoemitters has been studied in course of chemical treatment and thermal pyrolysis.

Fumed silica powder with specific surface area of about 295 m2/g was dispersed in toluene solution of PhTMS at the temperature of 70 оС for 4 h in the presence of triethylamine as a catalyst. Obtained products – phenylsilicas, were washed out with the dissolvent and dried at 80–100 oС. In the present work we have studied the effect of concentration of phenyl groups grafted to silica surface. For this purpose “low-carbon” series (“LC”) and “high-carbon” series (“HC”) were fabricated using concentration 0.52 ml and 1.73 ml of PhTMS per 10 ml of toluene, respectively. Surface modification was enhanced by adding of 0.02 ml triethylamine per 10 ml of toluene. Hereafter these samples will be identified as “LC phenylsilica” and “HC phenylsilica”. After chemical modification the phenylsilicas were thermally annealed at 600 °C in flow of pure nitrogen at atmospheric pressure for 30 min. Under thermal treatment, the colour of the powder changed from white to “milk” for LC series and brown for HC series (Fig. SI-1).

The dynamics of the atomic bonds restructuring and thermally induced reaction were studied by thermogravimetry (TG) analysis using the Q-1500D Paulik & Erdey derivatograph at atmospheric conditions. Typical thermogravimetric (TG) curves of the pristine fumed silica and LC and HC phenylsilicas are illustrated in Fig. SI-2. A dramatic weight loss of phenylsilicas at temperature above 500 °C is assigned to “combustion” of carbon in atmospheric oxygen. It can be seen that this weight loss in a HC series is about twice higher than that in LC.

Photoluminescence emission (PL) and excitation (PLE) were studied using SHIMADZU RF-1501 spectrophotometer. Our preliminary experiments revealed a strong effect of Rayleigh scattering in SiO2:C nanopowders that is manifested in PLE spectra as a background increasing rapidly with a decrease of wavelength in UV spectral range. This background hinders spectral features of excitation behaviour. It has been found in preliminary experiments that the effect of Raileigh scattering in PLE spectra is strongly reduced when nanopowder is dispersed in organic solution. Isopropyl alcohol showed good wettability to SiO2:C, therefore PL/PLE experiments in this work have been performed with SiO2:C powders dispersed in pure isopropyl that helps to examine details of PLE features.

The photo-chemical effect in SiO2:C powder was examined by set-up comprising HeCd (325 nm) laser as excitation source, a grating monochromator Jobin Yvon THR 1000, and a photomultiplier detection system (S1) operated at 77 K. Calibrated neutral-density filters were used to tune the excitation power density. Evolution of the PL spectra in air and in vacuum was studied using closed cycle He optical cryostat as vacuum vessel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of structure and chemical composition
ce:section.ce:para: Interatomic chemical bonds were studied by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) in transmission and diffusion scattering modes using Nicolet 6700/Nicolet Continuum (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Chemical composition and chemical state of surface atoms were analysed by photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using ULVAC PHI SUMMIT Model 1600 instrument. It should be noted that identification of the chemical state of the atoms using high resolution XPS spectra involves the analysis of the energy position and spectral contour of the peak. Characteristic chemical shifts are commonly of the order of few electronvolts therefore it is important to avoid the effect of electrical charging of the surface being analysed [17]. The charging effect caused by photoelectron emission is very critical for materials with poor conductivity, that is the case for SiO2:C nanocomposite. C1s core-level spectrum of adsorbed hydrocarbons is often used as a reference line to account for charging shifts [18,19], however such approach is not applicable for the analysis of carbonaceous materials. Elimination of charging effects in our XPS experiments was performed using following methods: (1) improving of the electrical contact with the sample holder by mechanical pressing of very small amount of the powder in soft indium foil, (2) compensation of negative charge losses by external electron flood gun (20 μA) and electrically grounded Ni mesh screen mounted about 1 mm above the sample surface.

Nanoscale morphology and chemical analysis were performed using aberration-corrected scanning electron transmission microscopy (AC-STEM) by a Jeol JEM ARM200F microscope, operated at 80 kV, equipped with a Gatan Energy Filter Quantum ER for electron energy-loss spectroscopy (EELS). To minimise irradiation beam damage, STEM experiments were performed at 100 K using a specimen cooling stage with liquid nitrogen.

Photoluminescence emission and excitation spectra of LC and HC phenylsilica samples are presented in Fig. 2 . Both samples exhibited almost similar emission and excitation bands centered at 327 nm and 288 nm, respectively. The only difference was a minor but well pronounced shoulder in the visible range at about 413 nm observed in HC series (Fig. 2d). Spectral contour and position of maximum of emission bands were independent of excitation wavelength (Fig. 2b,d).

Evolution of PL/PLE spectra after annealing was quite different in LC and HC series. LC sample exhibited the emission band with maximum intensity at about 380 nm with a significant shoulder in the visible spectral range (Fig. 3 b), while emission of HC sample dominated in the visible range with maximum intensity at about 440–445 nm (Fig. 3d). Excitation spectra of LC sample showed a prominent band at about 320–325 nm with long wavelength shoulder in the excitation spectra of visible emission (Fig. 3a). The emission band of HC sample was significantly broadened with maximum shifted to 345–355 nm (Fig. 3c).

Fig. 3e illustrates the spectral position of maximum intensity of emission bands in LC&HC series as a function of excitation wavelength λ EXC. It can be seen that the emission band of HC sample is excitation independent in the excitation range of 280–340 nm. An increase of λ EXC above 340 nm resulted in the small red shift up to about 450 nm. The emission band of LC series was excitation independent in the excitation range of 280–320 nm and was strongly shifted to about 420 nm when excitation wavelength being increased up to 360 nm (Fig. 3b). Detailed examination of PL band of LC sample measured under short wavelength excitation exhibited obvious paramount peak indicating multicomponent nature of the emission. Because of nonlinear relation between wavelength and photon energy the deconvolution of emission spectra into gauss components was performed in energy scale. The fitting procedure showed that the emission band can be well approximated by 4 G components with maximums at 3.7 eV (335 nm), 3.3 eV (375 nm), 3.0 eV (413 nm), and 2.8 eV (443 nm) (Fig. 3f). Minor contribution of high energy band (3.7 eV) can be assigned to the presence of phenyl groups that survived after annealing, while other bands are obviously a result of pyrolitical processes. Below it will be shown that presence of phenyl groups in annealed LC series is approved by FTIR measurements. Taking into account multi-component nature of the emission band it is reasonable now to assign a sharp excitation dependent shift of the emission (Fig. 3e) simply to reduction of relative contributions of the ultraviolet component with the increase of the excitation wavelength above the excitation range of this component.

In our previous study two characteristic PL bands were found in SiO2:C nanopowders synthesised at fixed phenyl groups incorporation (similar to that used in HC series in the current study) at different temperatures [15]. Pyrolysis at 500 °C resulted in development of 380 nm band, while increase of the annealing temperature up to 600 °C gave rise to 450 nm emission bands [15]. To link the previous study with the present work and to verify the effect of the annealing temperature, the “gradient temperature” annealing was carried out. The goal of the experiment was to find out whether PL band shifts monotonically in course of carbon precipitation or not. The main idea of the experiment is to prepare material that has been subjected to annealing temperature of wide range. The scheme of the experiment is illustrated in the Fig. SI-3: the long quartz boat with HC phenylsilica powder was positioned not in the area of constant temperature but on the edge of this zone, i.e. in the area of the temperature gradient. With such position the HC phenylsilica was treated by the temperature that is continuously decreased from 600 °C on one edge to about 100 °C on another edge of the boat. After annealing the powder has been thoroughly mixed and PL spectra of such mixture are presented in Fig. 4 a. It is clearly seen that PL spectrum under excitation of 280 nm is multicomponent with well distinguished two peaks and shoulders in ultraviolet and visible ranges. The spectrum can be well fitted in energy scale by four Gauss bands located at about 3.8 eV (326 nm), 3.4 eV (365 nm), 3.0 eV (413 nm) and 2.7 eV (459 nm) (Fig. 4b) that is in well agreement with deconvolution of the emission spectrum of annealed LC series (Fig. 3f). This result approves the existence of definite emission bands with characteristic spectral position.

At the end of the section of PL&PLE experiments it should be noted that all PL bands in all SiO2:C samples vanished below detection limits after annealing in oxygen or in air at 500–600 °C. Such oxidation quenching correlated with full removal of the carbon from fumed silica matrix approving that all emission bands were related to carbon moieties. Therefore, as a summary, it can be tabulated at least three spectrally separated characteristic PL bands that are originated from pyrolytic carbon and correlated with carbon concentration and annealing temperature.

Previously it was reported that PL in SiO2:C is bleaching under UV radiation at atmospheric conditions [15]. Therefore the PL/PLE studies presented in previous sections were performed using low-power excitation sources (Xe lamp) to minimise the photo-induced effects. For examination of the effects of UV radiation on PL properties the He–Cd laser was used in combination with optical filters for controllable variation of output 325 nm radiation power. Spectrum 1 in Fig. 5 a corresponds to PL of LC sample under minimal excitation power of 3 μW. The spectrum exhibits a primary peak at 380 nm and well defined shoulder at 420 nm quite similar to that of excited by Xe lamp (Fig. 3b). As the excitation power was increasing up to 0.4 mW the shoulder at 420 nm was smoothening and a new emission band with peak at about 495 nm appeared and developed with the further increase of the excitation power. After 2 h of experiments the radiation power has been returned to starting minimal value of 3 μW, however 495 nm emission band remained dominant (Fig. 5b, spectrum 2) indicating irreversible nature of the photo-induced effect. It should be noted that the intensity of PL signal was significantly reduced due to bleaching effect that can be seen from increased of noise/signal ratio. It is also important to point out that (i) new PL band was well spectrally separated with unshifted peak at 490–496 nm, (ii) appearance of this band correlated with vanishing of 420 nm shoulder, (iii) irreversible character of photoinduced transformation of emission spectrum is an evidence of structural reconstruction. In case of HC sample no effect of 325 nm laser radiation on the spectral distribution of emission was observed. The only effect of laser radiation in this sample was the decrease of PL efficiency.

As it was mentioned above, the PL in SiO2:C nanopowder sufferes from the bleaching under UV radiation. Similar bleaching effects were observed previously in the nanoporous SiO2:C layers [20]. However, this effect, being strong in air, was reduced or even completely avoided in nitrogen ambient or in vacuum [15,21]. Based on these observations, the bleaching effect in SiO2:C nanocomposites was attributed to photo-induced oxidation of carbon emission centers by analogy with bleaching effects well known in organic fluorophors. In the present study we performed the experiments for verification of the effect of the ambient on photo-induced bleaching. Two types of the measurements were performed. First, PL evolution was monitored in atmospheric air for time period of 1 h with step of 10 min under constant irradiation by 325 nm laser. Other measurements were performed under vacuum conditions: the sample was placed into evacuated chamber and PL was monitored starting from vacuum pumping switch-on. Evolution of integral PL intensity in HC sample in air and in vacuum is presented in Fig. 6 . Typical monotonous reduction of PL intensity was observed in atmospheric ambient (Fig. 6, curve 1), while in the vacuum the PL had dropped step-like right after pumping switch-on and no further degradation was observed (Fig. 6, curve 2). Step-like drop of PL intensity after evacuation is obviously related to desorption of some volatile species from the surface of the powder. After extracting of the sample from vacuum chamber the emission intensity was recovered within 1–2 days of exposure to air. Evolution of PL intensity in LC series was quite similar to that of HP sample both in air and in vacuum. These experiments point out the opposite effects of the air: (i) “destructive” effect of bleaching in air under UV radiation, and (ii) “constructive” effect of adsorption of some atmospheric components that significantly improves PL efficiency. At present time it is not possible to specify which of atmospheric components is affecting PL efficiency.

Interatomic bonds in SiO2:C powders were examined by FTIR spectroscopy in transmission and diffusion scattering modes. Survey FTIR transmission spectra of as-prepared and annealed phenylsilicas can be found in the supplementary information file (Fig. SI-4ab). Evolution of carbon related absorption bands (carbon-carbon and carbon-hydrogen) are illustrated in details in Fig. 7 .

Pristine fumed silica is hydrophilic material with large amount of absorbed moisture that was manifested in absorption bands at 3500 cm−1 due to O–H stretching vibration modes and 1630 cm−1 due to bending modes (spectra 1 in Fig. 7a and SI-4a). Chemically modified silica became hydrophobic with reduced amount of adsorbed moisture and correspondingly reduced intensity of O–H related absorption bands: CC (1436 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1) and C–H (2800-3100 cm−1) absorption bands are now dominating (Fig. 7, Fig. SI-4a). The narrow absorption band at 1594 cm−1 that is due to CC vibrations in benzene rings was identical in both LC and HC series (Fig. 7a, spectra 2,3). Annealing resulted in a partial recovery of hydrophilic property of nanopowder and O–H related bands were increased (Fig. 7, spectrum 4). However, well defined low frequency shoulder at about 1600 cm−1 due to CC absorption is well distinguished. In the spectrum of LC sample this shoulder is obviously composed of narrow and broad components. The sharp feature at 1594 cm−1 corresponds presumably to benzene rings still present in the material, while broad contribution of the shoulder can be assigned to disordered CC bonds (amorphous network). It can be seen that contribution from narrow component in the spectra of HC sample is smaller than in LC indicating stronger amorphization of carbon-carbon bonds.

Another spectral feature that appeared after chemical treatment was absorption multiplet at 2800-3100 cm−1 originated from C–H stretching modes (Fig. 7b, spectra 1,2). In the present study the identification of C–H multiplets can be limited by mentioning that absorption in range of 2800–3000 cm−1 corresponds to C (sp3)–H bonds in methoxy groups while absorption in range of 3000–3100 cm−1 is due to C (sp2)–H bonds in benzene ring [22]. After annealing no absorption in the range of 2800–3000 cm−1 was observed, indicating the effusion or destruction of methyl groups. Absorption in the range of 3000–3100 cm−1 was reduced strongly after annealing in both series. This band was well defined in the transmission spectrum of annealed LC series while it was weakly detectable in HC sample (Fig. 7b, spectra 3,4). This band was better defined in both series in FTIR spectra of diffusion scattering that is more sensitive to the surface species (Fig. SI-4c).

Chemical composition and interatomic chemical bonds were studied by XPS. Survey XPS spectra of pristine fumed silica as well as of annealed LC and HC samples are presented in Fig. SI-5a. Only silicon, carbon and oxygen peaks were observed in XPS spectra, as well as some traces of In originated from the sample holder. No nitrogen or other impurities were found. The ratio of C1s peaks in HC and LC samples was about 2:1 and well correlated with TG measurements (Fig. SI-2). High resolution C1s, O1s and Si2p peaks were analyzed for identification of chemical bonds (Fig. 8 ). C1s peaks of LC and HC samples presented in Fig. 8a and b were obviously asymmetric indicating multicomponent contributions. It is not a trivial task to make a direct and unambiguous link between spectral features of C1s peak and the chemical state of carbon atoms. However, taking into account chemical composition of the material it is reasonable to evaluate chemical shifts in terms of C–C/CC, C–O and C–Si bonds. Fitting procedure demonstrated that C1s peaks of both samples are well approximated by three components at 284 eV, 285 eV and 286 eV, that can be presumably assigned to CC double bonds, C–C single bonds and heteroatomic C–O single bonds, respectively [23]. The minor contribution from possibly present C–Si bonds (283 eV) cannot be excluded in the low energy shoulder but it was very small if present at all. Spectrum of LC samples exhibited a well defined π–π* shake-up satellite at about 291 eV (Fig. SI-5a). This characteristic feature is caused by energy loss on plasmon excitation in conjugated π-electron systems and is inherent both to inorganic graphite/graphene and organic materials [23, 24]. Shake-up satellite in the spectrum of HC sample was weaker and correlated with smaller contribution of low energy CC component in C1s peak. Dominant contribution in both spectra comes from 285 eV component that is due to disordered C–C bonds in frame of our consideration, It is suggested that this component originated from amorphous carbon precipitates. In HC series this component is stronger, indicating more intense carbon precipitation. High energy component of C1s peak (286 eV) is unambiguously attributed to carbon-oxygen single bonds because carbon-oxygen double bond has higher energy and usually is manifested as peak at 288–289 eV.

The analysis of carbon related contributions in Si2p and O1s peaks seems quite problematic because of dominant contribution from Si–O bonds of the silica host material. Si2p and O1s peaks were almost symmetric, however weak but well reproducible asymmetry of low energy side can be found in both spectra (Fig. 8c and d). Significant low energy shoulder in O1s spectrum is assigned to contribution of O–C bonds. In case of Si2p peak the low energy shoulder was very weak and can be attributed either to experimental artifact or to contribution of isolated Si–C bonds, for example, in O3 Si–C structural configuration.

Morphology of the pristine SiO2 and annealed SiO2:C nanopowders have been comprehensively studied by HRSTEM in our previous researches and presented in Ref. [15]. Morphology of pristine fumed silica was represented by SiO2 amorphous nanoparticles with diameter of about 10–20 nm fused together in larger agglomerates. As it was reported in our previous work, the nanoscale morphology of silica is not affected by chemical and/or thermal treatments [15]. The high stability of nanoscale morphology was confirmed by its stability under electron beam during several hours of STEM experiments. Electron diffraction analysis of SiO2:C showed no signs of crystalline components indicating fully amorphous structure of the material. No signs of separated three dimensional nanoparticles, which can be interpreted as carbon nanoparticles, were observed in annealed LC and HC samples (Fig. SI-6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon mapping and analysis of C–K edge
ce:section.ce:para: A spatial distribution of silicon, oxygen and carbon was examined by high resolution mapping of Si-L, O–K and C–K signal in EELS spectra. Fig. 9 illustrates distribution of the elements within selected area of the annealed HC sample. One can see that distribution of silicon and oxygen well replicates dark field image of the SiO2 matrix, while distribution of carbon is quite different and is represented by carbon enriched regions with size below 10 nm and dispersed over silica surface. Similar segregated carbon distribution was observed in LC series (Fig. SI-7). It can be concluded that carbon was precipitated on the surface of silica particles, probably in the form of thin layer spots. It is hard to say if these layers are continuous or discontinuous in subnanometer scale.

EELS spectra of C–K ionisation edge in SiO2:C samples are presented in Fig. 10 . Spectra of both LC and HC series have some common characteristic features such as the narrow peak at 285 eV and the broad edge at 290 eV that are due to π* and σ* ionisation, respectively. However, some distinct deviations are worth to be pointed out. First, the spectrum of LC series has more pronounced σ*-peak at about 292 eV (Fig. 10, spectrum 1) while in the spectra of HC sample this feature is blurred (Fig. 10, spectrum 2). Second, π*-peak and σ*-edge in HC sample is shifted by about 0.4 eV to the higher energy in comparison with LC sample. These observations can be interpreted as an evidence of more ordered CC bonds in LC series and higher fraction of disordered amorphous carbon in HC series that is well consistent with previous interpretation of FTIR and XPS data.

For better evaluation of the characteristic features of the C–K spectrum graphene oxide (GO) flake was examined using the same equipment and experimental conditions (Fig. 10, spectrum 3). GO was fabricated by modified Hummers' method and contains about 35 at.% of chemically bonded oxygen [25]. Analysed flake has a thickness of few graphene layers. Spectrum of GO contains well defined π*- and σ*- peak as well as additional peak at 286.6 eV that is attributed to carbon-oxygen bonds. From the comparison of the spectra of GO and SiO2:C in the range of 286–288 eV one can expect a contribution of carbon-oxygen bonds both in LC and HC series that is well consistent with the XPS data.

Thus correlation of FTIR, XPS and EELS data provides a well grounded conclusion that carbon nanoprecipitates in both series show primarily amorphous hybrides of sp2/sp3 carbon with the evidence of pi-conjugated atomic systems that is dominant in LC and minor in HC series.

The analysis of PL properties of annealed SiO2:C nanopowders was performed in frame of the concept of luminescent CDs by analogy with our previous studies [14–16]. It is assumed that the evolution of PL properties reflects the processes of the thermolysis of phenyl groups and precipitation of carbon on the surface of silica nanoparticles. Despite numerous researches on synthesis and optical properties of CDs the mechanism of effective and spectrally tuneable light emission is still under debates and there is no commonly accepted consensus at the moment. What is the structure of light emission centers and what are CDs at all? Are there some similarity with semiconductor quantum dots or is it more relevant to organic phluorophores?

As-prepared LC and HC phenylsilicas demonstrated well defined and excitation independent PL band at 330 nm and corresponding PLE band and 288 nm (Fig. 2). Similar emission and excitation bands were observed in phenylsiloxane polymers [26]. Formation of phenylsiloxane complexes on the surface of fumed silica after treatment by PhTMS was observed experimentally by other researchers [27]. Taking into account spectroscopic fingerprints of phenyl groups in FTIR spectra (Fig. 7), UV emission band at 330 nm has to be unambiguously assigned to phenyl groups. Pronounced shoulder in the emission band of HC phenylsilica (Fig. 2d) can be also assigned to the surface complexes, because similar fluorescence band was observed in the silicon–organic polymers with phenyl substitutions [28.29] and has been assigned to the defects in silicon-organic chains. HC sample contains high surface concentration of silil groups that presumably enhances the formation of defective polymer-like chains with attached phenyl groups. Such identification seems quite reasonable because no visible shoulder was observed in the LC sample with lower concentration of Phenyl-siloxane groups.

Annealing resulted in development of near-UV emission band at about 380 nm in LC series and visible broad band with maximum intensity at about 440–450 nm in HC series (Fig. 3b,c). It is important to note that these near-UV and broad visible emission bands are quite similar to the emission bands previously observed in annealed phenylsilicas with given carbon concentration (similar to that of HC series in the present study) but annealed at different temperatures [15]: the near-UV band with a maximum of 380 nm dominated after annealing at 500 °C, while a broad visible band with a maximum at about 450 nm was observed after annealing at 600 °C. Similar two-component PL bands were observed also in annealed fumed silica with surface terminated by methoxy [14] and methyl [30] groups indicating that multiband nature of PL emission in SiO2:C nanocomposites is independent of carbon precursor. However, the detailed analysis of emission band in annealed LC series and the experiment with “gradient annealing” of HC series in our present study demonstrated that actually there are two characteristic emission bands at about 360–380 nm and 410–420 nm respectively that are strongly overlapped but spectrally separated (Figs. 3f and 4b). Under intense UV laser radiation 410–420 band is converted into emission band with maximum at 495 nm (Fig. 5). Increase of the annealing temperature above 600 °C resulted in gradual red shift and quenching of emission band both in LC and HC series. Typical PL spectra of HC series annealed at 650 °C and 700 °C are illustrated in Fig. SI-8.

As a summary it is suggested that three types of characteristic emission bands were observed in the annealed SiO2:C nanopowder. Centers of type I and II are formed at a lower temperature and/or at a lower concentration of carbon, and these centers emit in the near UV and violet spectral ranges with relatively fixed “gravity center” around 360–380 nm and 410–420 nm, respectively. High carbon concentration and/or high annealing temperature resulted in development of broad visible emission band of type III with maximum intensity tuneable in a broad spectral range above 440 nm. Due to large width of the bands in comparison to spectral distance between them they are overlapped. Photoinduced emission band at 495 nm (type IV) will be discussed below.

Structural identification of the carbon related emission centers responsible for the characteristic emission bands is not a trivial problem, and first it is worth to make some general notes. In this discussion we are examining pyrolytic carbon precipitates in SiO2:C nanocomposite by analogy with luminescent CDs that are commonly associated with three dimensional carbon nanoparticles of few nanometers. It was demonstrated that individual carbon nanoparticle exhibited a well homogeneous emission band with monoexponential decay and behaves as a single emitter while ensembles of the particles show a broad emission and nonexponential PL decay as a result of superposition of the emissions from different emitters [31]. However, it has been found that linear dimensions of the “optically active” light emitters in CDs is much smaller than the size of carbon nanoparticles observed by HRTEM [32,33]. Another research group suggested the model of luminescent CDs as “molecular cocktail” composed by polycyclic molecular fluorophors like antracene, pyrene, perylene etc. [34] that is in line with other researches [35]. According to these studies the individual light emitters are identified rather as subnanometer-sized molecular-like moieties (functional group, clusters) bound to the surface of nanometer-sized carbon particles, i.e. carbon nanoparticle can be considered as a “carrier” of the emitter but not as an emitter by itself. The concept of carbon polycyclic molecular-like clusters as light emitters is well consistent with our experimental results. First, HRSTEM studies did not reveal any 3D carbon nanoparticles after thermal annealing. Second, FTIR and XPS studies reveal the presence of conjugated carbon structures. The strong effect of adsorption/desorption on the PL intensity points out the surface nature of the light emitters rather than bulk centers (Fig. 6).

It is reasonable to suggest that there are two general roots of pyrolytic reconstruction of phenyl groups: (i) “destruction root” - decomposition of phenyl groups and formation of amorphous carbon precipitates; (ii) “construction root” – fusing of carbon moieties into polycyclic structures, similar to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon molecules (PAH). As far as the formation of polycyclic clusters is concerned this process should follow successive growth of the number of hexagon rings i.e. bi-rings, three-rings, etc. It is known that bi-ring armatic fluorophors (naphthalene, bi-phenyl) emit in near-UV spectral range, while three-ring fluorophors (antracene, phenanthrene) are characterised by PL in violet-blue. With increase of the number of rings emission band in PAH is shifted to long wavelength [36]. At lower temperature and/or lower surface concentration of phenyl groups it is expected predominant formation of bi-ring structures. The discrete increase in the conjugation energy with transformation of single-ring to bi-ring structure results in the discrete low energy spectral shift of the emission band from 330 nm to 360–380 nm. With increasing the temperature and/or concentration of carbon precursors, the average size of carbon clusters tends to increase resulting in domination of three-ring clusters and the corresponding discrete red shift of emission to 410–420 nm. The main idea of our hypothesis is that discrete change of the number of rings in polycyclic structures that are presumably formed during annealing is responsible for spectral separation and discrete spectral shift of the emission bands. Further intensification of carbon precipitation with increase of the temperature and/or the carbon concentration results in enhancement of aggregation processes and domination of amorphous fraction with corresponding red shift and PL quenching [37]. A final result of carbon precipitation at higher temperature is “graphitization” of the local structure and formation of nonluminescent soot [38].

Photo-induced green emission band (Fig. 5) now should be identified in frame of hypothesis on polycyclic molecular-like carbon clusters. It is important to note that appearance and development of green emission band is correlated with the decrease of 420 nm shoulder of dominating UV band (Fig. 5a). In previous section this shoulder has been assigned preliminary to 3-ring polycyclic fluorophors. Therefore it is reasonable to assign the development of green emission band to photo-activated structural modification of blue emitting centers. Similar irreversible photo-induced effect of the appearance of a broad, featureless emission at energies well below the normal “monomeric” fluorescence bands was observed in fluorescence spectra of solid state naphthalene, anthracene, and phenanthrene subjected to high pressure in combination with UV radiation [39]. The effect was attributed to photo-activated reorientation of nearby pairs of emitters in an excimer-like mutual position resulting in activation of “excimeric” emission. Polyciclic fluorophors on the silica surface are supposed to have sufficient freedom of motion and can be easily reoriented under local heating by intense UV radiation. Therefore, we suggest that similar “excimeric” mechanism of conversion of short wavelength “monomeric” emission into long wavelength “excimeric” emission can be valid in our case.

Alternative explanation is photo/thermo activated structural reconstruction with formation of covalent bonds between nearby fluorophors. Similar effect of conversion of “monomeric” blue emission into “excimeric” green emission was observed also in solid state organic compounds that contain two anthracene fluorophors bonded by either conjugated or nonconjugated bridge [40]. Contribution of photo/thermo activated aggregation of fluorophors is also expected in this effect.

Thus, the four types of spectrally separated PL bands have been found in as-prepared, annealed and UV irradiated phenylsilicas. Corresponding structural/energy model is illustrated in Fig. 11 . The concept of few-ring polyciclic carbon fluorophors is well consistent with PL properties of 2,3-ring polycyclic organic fluorophors. However, such a concept gives the rise of the question about the detailed structure of the fluorophors: is there some difference in the structure of carbon fluorophors in SiO2:C composite and corresponding organic molecular counterparts like naphthalene and anthracene ? The high synthesis temperature is hardily compatible with “classical” organic molecules and implies higher structural stability of the fluorophors. It can be suggested that carbon fluorophors are covalently bonded to amorphous carbon precipitates or to the silica surface presumably through C–O–C or C–O–Si bridges respectively that is in good agreement with XPS evidence of the contribution from C–O bonds in C1s spectra (Fig. 8).

Luminescent SiO2:C nanopowders have been synthesised by chemical treatment of fumed silica with phenyltrimethoxysilane followed by thermal annealing at 600 °C. Two series of SiO2:C with different concentration of carbon precursor were studied. Evolution the photoluminescence as a result of pyrolysis as well as photo-induced effects were studied. Four characteristic emission bands in UV and VIS spectral range (360-38-0 nm, 410–420 nm, 440–450 nm, 495 nm) were found in annealed SiO2:C. Correlation of photoluminescence and structural properties of carbon nanoprecipitates was analysed and discussed in frame of the approach to pyrolytic process as combination of “constructive” formation of polycyclic molecular-like carbon clusters and “destructive” aggregation of carbon into amorphous network. It is suggested to assign the characteristic emission bands at about 380 nm and 420 nm to polycyclic carbon fluorophors with 2- and 3-ring structure, respectively. Other low energy characteristic emission bands at wavelength 440–450 nm and higher are assigned to amorphous carbon aggregates with possible contribution from excimers of polyciclic fluorophors. Significant effects of adsorption/desorption in air on the photoluminescence intensity was observed for the first time.

It was shown that fumed silica with high specific surface area can be used as solid state “container” for pyrolytic synthesis of carbon nanoemitters with emission spectrum variable from the UV to the visible. The relatively high temperature of the synthesis of SiO2:C nanocomposite ensures its improved thermal stability, that is an advantage as compared to carbon dots synthesised by low-temperature wet chemistry processes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have declared that no conflicting interests exist.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Science&Technology Center in Ukraine (Project #6362) and Ministry of Education and Science of Ukraine (Project F2904). One of the authors (AVV) acknowledges the 27th (2018) DAIKO ZAIDAN Scholarship for the international collaboration program by inviting foreign researchers to Japanese research institutes. This work was in part supported by Nagoya University microstructural characterization platform as a program of “Nanotechnology Platform” of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT), Japan.

Infrared, Raman and magnetic resonance spectroscopic study of SiO2:C nanopowders, Nanoscale Res. Lett. 12, 292. https://doi.org/10.1186/s11671-017-2057-1.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excimer emission of naphthalene, anthracene, and phenanthrene crystals produced by very high pressures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.F. Jones, M. Nicol, Excimer emission of naphthalene, anthracene, and phenanthrene crystals produced by very high pressures, Jour. Chem. Phys. 48(12) (1968) 5440-5447. https://doi.org/10.1063/1.1668237.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and characterization of new anthracene-based semi-conducting materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kh. Hriz, N. Jaballah, J.-L. Fave, M. Majdoub, Synthesis and characterization of new anthracene-based semi-conducting materials, J. Mater. Sci. 47 (2012) 8067-8075. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10853-012-6699-1.
